caption: Figure 1. Semi-supervised vision-language alignment. We tackle the alignment of frozen unimodal encoders where paired data (red blocks) are scarce but unpaired data are abundant. The key challenge is: how to define a training signal for unpaired data when ground-truth cross-modal correspondences are

Figure 2. SOTAlign is a two-step method for the alignment of pretrained unimodal image and text encoders. First, we fit a linear alignment model only using the limited amount of available image-text pairs. Then, we use this linear model as a teacher to regularize the training of alignment layers f a

Figure 3. GPU memory usage for a batchsize n = 10k when computing the gradient of the OT-based divergence with naive solver unrolling (blue) and the provided explicit gradient formula (orange). Additional results are reported in Appendix B.1.
Figure 4. We plot Sinkhorn convergence criterion ||P1−1||1 after 100 iterations against ϵ. The cost matrix of size 32k used for this plot is extracted from a SOTAlign training batch.

Figure 5. Left: Effect of the number of paired samples (while fixing 1M unpaired samples). Right: Effect of the number of unpaired samples (while fixing 10k pairs). We report the zero-shot retrieval (MeanR@1) on COCO. More metrics are reported in Appendix B.

Figure 6. Relationship between the total sliced Wasserstein dis- tance between CC3M image/text dataset and unimodal datasets, and the downstream performance of SOTAlign trained on 10k CC3M image–text pairs and up to 1M samples from the corre- sponding unimodal datasets. As a baseline, we consider ra
Figure 7. Relationship between the representational similarity (mu- tual kNN) of frozen encoder pairs and their retrieval performance after semi-supervised alignment using SOTAlign.

Figure 8. Comparison of memory usage, runtime, and number of Sinkhorn iterations for different gradient computation strategies. We run as many Sinkhorn iterations as required to achieve marginal convergence within a tolerance of 10−6.
Figure 9. Effect of the number of paired samples during alignment on downstream zero-shot classification and retrieval. We fix 1M unpaired samples from CC3M and vary the number of paired samples.
Figure 10. Effect of the number of unpaired samples during alignment on downstream zero-shot classification and retrieval. We fix 10k paired samples from CC3M and vary the number of unpaired samples.
Figure 11. Sensitivity of SOTAlign to the regularization weight α under varying supervision regimes (number of supervised pairs) and varying noise level of the unpaired data.
Figure 12. OT regularization. We fix α = 0.001, use 1M unpaired samples, and vary ϵ and ϵ∗across different supervision regimes.

Figure 13. Performance when using CC3M as paired data, CC3M text as unpaired text, and other image datasets as unpaired images, together with a comparison to the spherical sliced Wasserstein distance between CC3M image and the other image datasets.

Figure 14. Performance when using CC3M as paired data, CC3M text as unpaired text, and other image datasets as unpaired images, together with a comparison to the spherical sliced Wasserstein distance between CC3M image and the other image datasets.
Figure 15. Spherical sliced Wasserstein distances between different image datasets (left) and text datasets (right). We report mean and std of the distances over 20 seeds.
Figure 16. Satellite image-text retrieval on SkyScript with 10k test pairs.
Figure 17. Satellite image-text retrieval on SkyScript with 30k test pairs.
